whatToFeed: Balanced, water-soluble organic liquid fertilizer or bloom booster.
pruningTips: Pinch off spent flower clusters and yellowing leaves regularly to encourage non-stop blooming and keep the plant bushy.
flavorProfile: Mildly tart and citrus-like; flowers are technically edible but primarily grown for visual appeal.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant outdoors mid-to-late May once all danger of frost has passed and soil is warm.
whenToHarvest: Harvest flowers continuously if using for culinary garnishes or arrangements.
usesForLargeHarvests: Edible flower garnishes for salads, pressing flowers for crafts, container arrangements.
hardinessNote: Grows strictly as a frost-sensitive annual in Zone 6/7.
plantingInstructions: Gently remove the seedling from its nursery container and plant it in a hole matching the depth of the root ball. Space multiple plants 8-12 inches apart. Press the surrounding soil down lightly and water thoroughly around the base.
growthRequirements: Prefers rich, loose, highly porous organic soil with excellent drainage and a slightly acidic pH of 5.5 to 6.5. Requires steady moisture but is highly susceptible to root and stem rot if left in soggy, waterlogged dirt.

whatToFeed: Slow-release balanced organic granular fertilizer applied in spring and mid-summer.
pruningTips: Pruning is minimal; simply pull or snip off older, ragged leaves at the very base of the stalk to keep the central spike looking clean.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Grown strictly as an architectural foliage accent plant.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Move outdoors mid-to-late May when overnight temperatures stay reliably above 50°F.
whenToHarvest: Not applicable; enjoyed for its continuous architectural sword-like leaf structure all season long.
usesForLargeHarvests: Stunning central 'thriller' component in large patio containers and tropical garden bed displays.
hardinessNote: Grows as an annual in Zone 6/7. Can be potted and brought indoors as a houseplant before the first hard autumn freeze.
plantingInstructions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the nursery pot. Place the plant so the top of the root ball is level with the soil surface. Backfill with a blend of native soil and organic compost, firming it gently to anchor the tall structure.
growthRequirements: Thrives in fertile, well-aerated, and sharply-draining loam with a neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Quite drought-tolerant once established; avoid overwatering. Requires bright sun to maintain its deep, rich burgundy-red coloration.

whatToFeed: Low-nitrogen organic liquid fertilizer; thrives well in average, low-fertility conditions.
pruningTips: Cut back the plant by half in mid-summer if it becomes leggy or starts producing its small, inconspicuous yellow flowers, forcing fresh silver felt leaves.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Grown strictly for its striking, velvety silver foliage textures.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant outdoors in late April or early May; highly resilient to early spring chilly weather snaps.
whenToHarvest: Cut down the striking silver felt leaf stems at any time throughout the season for flower arrangements.
usesForLargeHarvests: Excellent accent textures in cut floral arrangements, holiday winter wreath accents, border edging.
hardinessNote: Grows as an annual in Zone 6/7, but can sometimes survive mild Western PA winters under heavy leaf mulch.
plantingInstructions: Set out young transplants 8-10 inches apart in a sunny garden bed or container edge. Ensure the plant is set at the same depth it was in the nursery container, pack the soil down, and water lightly to settle roots.
growthRequirements: Demands light, gritty, or sandy soils with superb drainage and a neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Highly drought-resistant once its roots are anchored. Requires full sun; shade causes it to lose its white velvet hair coatings and turn green.

whatToFeed: Low-nitrogen organic fertilizer or bone meal in early spring and again right after the blooming cycle ends; avoid high-nitrogen feeds which trigger rhizome rot.
pruningTips: Cut down the flower stalks completely to the base after blooming finishes to prevent seed development. In late autumn, trim the sword-like fan leaves down to a 4-6 inch angled wedge to discourage overwintering pests like the iris borer.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Extremely toxic to humans and pets.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant bare-root rhizomes from late July through September to ensure root establishment before the winter freeze.
whenToHarvest: Cut long, elegant flowering spikes in spring when the first bud on the stem is just beginning to show color but has not fully opened.
usesForLargeHarvests: Stunning spring cut flower arrangements, rhizome division for gifting to neighbors.
hardinessNote: Reliable, extremely hardy perennial in Zone 6/7; requires a cold winter dormancy period to trigger spring blooms.
plantingInstructions: Dig a shallow hole, build a small ridge of soil in the center, and place the horizontal rhizome on top, spreading the roots down the sides. Crucially, backfill so the top of the thick rhizome remains partially exposed to the sun and air like a duck's back. Space 12-24 inches apart.
growthRequirements: Demands exceptionally well-draining, fertile, light loam or gritty soil with a neutral pH of 6.8 to 7.2. Rhizomes must never sit in standing water or damp mulch, as they rot rapidly. Requires absolute full sun all day to bloom successfully.

sunRequirement: Full Sun to Bright Indirect Light
whatToFeed: Organic liquid succulent fertilizer diluted to half strength during the spring and summer active growth phases.
pruningTips: Pinch off spent flower clusters down to the next leaf node after blooms fade to stimulate new bud development and maintain a compact, bushy form.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Mildly toxic to household pets.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Move outdoors late May after all threat of frost passes, or grow indoors year-round as a houseplant.
whenToHarvest: Cut full flowering stems for small indoor arrangements when buds are 50% open.
usesForLargeHarvests: Long-lasting indoor potted color displays, bright outdoor patio container arrangements, desktop floral gifts.
hardinessNote: Grows as a frost-sensitive annual or patio plant in Zone 6/7; must be brought indoors before temperatures drop below 50°F.
plantingInstructions: Gently loosen the plant from its container and plant it in a well-draining pot or bed at the exact same depth it was growing. If planting in a container, ensure there are ample drainage holes at the base to prevent stagnant water.
growthRequirements: Demands extremely loose, gritty, sharply draining cactus or succulent potting mix with a pH of 6.0 to 6.5. Requires the soil to dry out completely between watering intervals. Needs highly bright, sunny exposures to bloom successfully.

whatToFeed: Slow-release bulb fertilizer or bone meal incorporated into the soil during autumn planting and scratched into the surface in very early spring.
pruningTips: Cut down the spent green flower stalks after blooming ends to prevent seed production. Crucially, leave the green foliage completely intact until it naturally turns yellow and dies back in early summer, as the leaves recharge the bulb.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Highly toxic to humans and household pets.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant bulbs outdoors in autumn (mid-to-late October) before the ground freezes.
whenToHarvest: Cut flowers in early spring when the buds are in the 'goose-neck' stage, showing full color but right before opening.
usesForLargeHarvests: Stunning early-spring cut flower arrangements, naturalizing large drifts in lawns or woodland borders.
hardinessNote: Reliable, extremely hardy perennial bulb in Zone 6/7; naturally repels deer, rabbits, and rodents.
plantingInstructions: Dig a hole 6 inches deep (roughly three times the height of the bulb). Place the bulb in the hole with the pointed end facing upward and the flat root end facing downward. Space bulbs 4-6 inches apart and backfill with loose soil.
growthRequirements: Thrives in average, well-draining, crumbly soil with a neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires consistent moisture throughout the active spring growing and blooming period, but must have dry conditions during summer dormancy.

whatToFeed: Light dusting of bone meal or low-nitrogen bulb food in autumn and when early spring tips emerge.
pruningTips: Deadhead faded blooms quickly. Let the short, narrow foliage mature and wither naturally over 6 weeks post-bloom before clearing it away.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Highly toxic to humans and pets.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant miniature bulbs 4 inches deep in October for early spring color.
whenToHarvest: Snip small stems just as the diminutive yellow cups begin to unfurl.
usesForLargeHarvests: Excellent for border edging, rock gardens, small spring window boxes, and petite tabletop flower arrangements.
hardinessNote: Perennial; multiplies rapidly over time to form dense, cheerful spring flower clumps.
plantingInstructions: Dig a small trench or individual holes 4 inches deep. Position the miniature bulbs pointed-side up, spacing them 2-3 inches apart. Cover with loose soil and water thoroughly once to settle the bulbs for winter.
growthRequirements: Requires loose, sandy loam with exceptional drainage and a pH range of 6.0 to 7.0. Benefits from bright early spring sun before deciduous trees leaf out completely. Avoid waterlogged soils, which cause bulb rot.

whatToFeed: Balanced bulb fertilizer high in phosphorus applied during fall planting.
pruningTips: Clip off spent flower heads at the top of the stem. Allow the long straps of foliage to remain intact until completely withered to feed next year's embryonic bloom.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Highly toxic to humans and pets.
combativePlants: Shade trees that block early spring light
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in mid-to-late autumn.
whenToHarvest: Cut stems on an angle when the distinct coral-pink rimmed central cup is fully displayed.
usesForLargeHarvests: Unique bi-color accents in mid-spring floral bouquets, striking container focal points.
hardinessNote: Perennial; the unique pink coloration of the central cup develops best in cool, bright spring weather.
plantingInstructions: Set large bulbs 6 inches deep into well-loosened soil, keeping a distance of 4-6 inches between each bulb. Ensure the pointed growth tip is facing upright, backfill firmly with dirt, and mulch lightly to preserve winter moisture.
growthRequirements: Thrives in fertile, organic-rich, well-aerated loam with a pH of 6.5 to 7.0. Demands excellent spring drainage. Prefers bright sun to fully develop its unique pink hues, but can tolerate dappled light.

whatToFeed: Heavy feeder; apply organic vegetable or flower fertilizer high in phosphorus and potassium once the plant reaches 3 feet tall.
pruningTips: Pruning is generally unnecessary for giant single-stem varieties. You may need to support the heavy, maturing flower heads by staking them to a sturdy post.
flavorProfile: Seeds have a nutty, rich flavor when roasted; petals are edible but have a bittersweet herbal accent.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow seeds outdoors in mid-to-late May once all frost threat has passed and soil reaches 60°F.
whenToHarvest: Harvest seed heads in autumn when the back of the green flower disk turns yellow-brown and the seeds look plump and loose.
usesForLargeHarvests: Roasting edible sunflower seeds, harvesting fresh cut flowers for tall vases, leaving seed heads to feed backyard wild birds.
hardinessNote: Fast-growing, heat-loving annual; completes its entire life cycle in a single summer season.
plantingInstructions: Sow large seeds directly into the garden 1 inch deep, spacing them 6 inches apart in a row. Once the seedlings grow 6 inches tall, thin out the weaker sprouts to leave a final spacing of 12-18 inches between giant stalks.
growthRequirements: Requires deeply cultivated, loose, fertile soil with good drainage and a neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.5. Develops long taproots that need deep ground space. Demands absolute maximum full sun exposure and protection from high winds.
